name: AWS Cognito API
what_it_does: AWS Cognito provides user pool management and authentication APIs enabling agents to create and manage users, groups, and app clients, trigger authentication flows, manage MFA, and federate identities for AWS service access via identity pools.
use_cases: Programmatically create user pool accounts and add users to groups as part of an automated onboarding workflow
Initiate admin authentication flows to generate tokens for downstream service calls in a backend automation pipeline
List or disable user accounts based on policy rules (e.g., disable inactive users after 90 days)
Update user attributes (email, custom attributes) in bulk when synchronizing with an authoritative user directory
Configure and manage app clients and their OAuth2 scopes when onboarding new application integrations to the user pool
not_for: Custom authentication UI — Cognito's hosted UI is limited; building rich custom login flows requires significant workaround effort
Complex enterprise identity federation (on-premises AD, SAML) at scale — Cognito SAML federation has significant limitations versus dedicated IAM products
Multi-tenant SaaS identity management with per-tenant customization — Cognito user pools are not designed for tenant isolation at scale
best_when: An agent manages user authentication and identity for applications already built on AWS, where tight IAM integration and SDK availability outweigh the feature limitations of Cognito versus dedicated IAM platforms.
avoid_when: You need advanced enterprise IAM features like fine-grained delegated administration, complex SAML SP-initiated flows, or per-tenant branding — dedicated identity platforms handle these far better.

auth.notes: Admin API operations require AWS IAM credentials (SigV4 signed requests) with appropriate Cognito IAM policies. User-facing auth endpoints (InitiateAuth, RespondToAuthChallenge) do not require IAM auth but require a client ID. The admin API (AdminInitiateAuth, AdminCreateUser) uses IAM auth. SRP (Secure Remote Password) protocol is used for password-based flows.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 50,000 monthly active users (MAUs) free per user pool; identity pools are free for the first 50K authentications/month
pricing.paid_tiers: $0.0055 per MAU for 50K–100K users; tiered pricing decreasing at higher volumes; SAML/OIDC federation costs extra at $0.015 per MAU
pricing.notes: Costs scale with MAUs rather than total users, making it cost-effective for apps with large registered but less active user bases. SAML federation significantly increases per-MAU cost.

performance.rate_limits: Admin API: 10–50 requests/second depending on operation (AdminGetUser: 25/s, AdminCreateUser: 10/s); user-facing auth: 120 requests/second per user pool; limits can be raised via AWS support

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Cognito returns typed exceptions (UserNotFoundException, NotAuthorizedException, etc.) via the AWS SDK, which are descriptive and easy to handle programmatically. Raw HTTP responses are less informative; SDK usage is strongly preferred.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: AdminCreateUser is not idempotent — creating a user who already exists throws UsernameExistsException. AdminUpdateUserAttributes is idempotent. Auth flows are stateful and not idempotent by design.
known_agent_gotchas: Admin API and user-facing API have different rate limits — mixing admin operations with user-facing flows in one agent can cause unexpected throttling on different limit pools
SRP authentication requires a multi-step challenge-response flow — agents must implement multiple round trips (InitiateAuth → RespondToAuthChallenge) rather than a single credential submission
Custom attributes must be declared at user pool creation and cannot be removed later — schema changes require creating a new user pool and migrating users
Cognito tokens (access, ID, refresh) have different scopes and lifetimes; agents using tokens for downstream AWS services must use ID tokens, not access tokens, for user attribute claims
User pool triggers (Lambda pre/post auth, pre sign-up) can silently block operations if the Lambda throws an unhandled error — debugging requires CloudWatch log correlation

security_score_components.security_notes: Admin API protected by AWS IAM SigV4 signing provides strong auth. Cognito supports MFA (TOTP, SMS), advanced security mode for risk-based auth, and token revocation. User pool passwords are never exposed via API.
